example.name: responsive_dashboard_redesign
example.user_request: "Our analytics dashboard is unusable on mobile. We need a redesign that works for both field workers checking numbers on phones and analysts doing deep-dive on desktop."

step.approach: - Primary users: Field workers (mobile-first, quick scans) and Analysts (desktop, deep analysis) - Environment: Field workers use phones outdoors; analysts use desktops in office - Key metrics: Time-to-insight for field workers; flexibility for analysts - Constraint: Existing data API cannot change
step.expected_outcome: Clear user personas and their distinct needs defined

step.approach: - Mobile: Card-based KPI summary with drill-down on tap - Desktop: Multi-panel with persistent filters and expandable charts - Navigation: Bottom tab bar (mobile) → Top nav with dropdowns (desktop) - Information architecture based on user task frequency
step.expected_outcome: Interaction model and navigation logic documented

step.approach:
Mobile (under 768px): - Single column, stacked cards - Each KPI card shows primary metric + sparkline - Tap card to expand details (bottom sheet) - Sticky header with date range selector
Tablet (768px - 1024px): - Two-column grid for KPIs - Charts can be expanded to full-width - Collapsible filter sidebar
Desktop (1024px and above): - Persistent left sidebar with filter tree - Main area: 3-column KPI grid - Right panel: Detail inspector (can be pinned) - Multi-window support for analysts

Touch (Mobile): - Tap KPI card → Expand bottom sheet with chart + breakdown - Swipe between time periods - Long-press for quick actions (share, annotate) - Pull to refresh data
Mouse (Desktop): - Hover on KPI → Tooltip with comparison period - Click → Expand in right inspector panel - Right-click → Context menu (export, annotate, compare) - Keyboard: Arrow keys navigate KPIs, Enter expands
Gestures: - No complex gestures; simple tap/click suffices - Pinch-to-zoom on charts (desktop)
step.expected_outcome: Touch and mouse interaction models defined

step.approach:
UI Library Decision: shadcn/ui with custom chart wrappers Reasoning: Standard table, card, and dialog patterns from shadcn/ui work well. Charts need custom wrappers for the existing charting library.
Components: - KPICard: Custom component with loading skeleton - DataTable: shadcn/ui Table with virtual scrolling for large datasets - FilterPanel: Collapsible accordion using shadcn/ui - Chart: Custom wrapper around chart library - BottomSheet: shadcn/ui Dialog customized for mobile - InspectorPanel: Custom resizeable panel component
State Management: - URL state for filters (shareable, bookmarkable) - Local state for panel open/closed - Server state with React Query for data
step.expected_outcome: Component architecture with UI library decisions

step.approach: - Typography: System font stack, 14px base on desktop, 16px on mobile - Spacing: 4px grid, 8px base unit - Density: Comfortable on mobile, compact on desktop - Colors: High contrast for outdoor visibility (field workers) - Charts: Color-blind safe palette - Icons: Lucide icons, 20px on mobile, 16px on desktop

- WCAG AA contrast for all text - Focus visible states on all interactive elements - Screen reader announcements for data updates - Keyboard navigation complete on desktop - Touch targets: 48x48px minimum on mobile - Error states: Inline validation with clear messages

- Use CSS Grid for main layout, Flexbox for components - Implement responsive layout with CSS container queries where possible - Virtual scrolling for tables with 100+ rows - React Suspense boundaries around chart components - Mobile: Prevent body scroll when bottom sheet is open - Performance: Lazy load charts below the fold

Risk 1: Dense desktop view may not translate well to tablet Mitigation: Test tablet layout early; consider tablet-specific optimizations
Risk 2: Field workers may need offline capability Mitigation: Add PWA service worker for read-only offline mode
Risk 3: Chart library may not be responsive Mitigation: Wrap in custom responsive container with ResizeObserver
Risk 4: Complex filters may overwhelm field workers Mitigation: Show essential filters on mobile, full filter panel on desktop

example.completion: Designed a responsive analytics dashboard that serves both mobile field workers (card-based quick scans) and desktop analysts (dense multi-panel interface). Provided breakpoint-specific layouts, interaction models for touch and mouse, component architecture using shadcn/ui with custom chart wrappers, and identified risks around tablet layout and offline capability.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Mobile-first forces prioritization; start with what field workers need most
Breakpoints should be defined by content needs, not device categories
UI library decision should be explicit and based on specific needs

example.name: touch_friendly_canvas_editor
example.scenario: Design a touch-friendly canvas editor for creating floor plans on tablets. Users draw rectangles, place pins, and connect elements with lines. Must work on iPad Pro with Apple Pencil and keyboard accessories.
example.user_request: "We need a canvas editor that lets users draw floor plans on tablets. It needs to be touch-first but also support keyboard shortcuts for power users."

step.approach: - Primary users: Facility managers creating floor plans on tablets - Environment: Office or on-site with tablet (iPad Pro focus) - Key interactions: Draw shapes, move elements, connect with lines, annotate - Constraints: Must work with Apple Pencil, keyboard accessories

- Canvas-first interaction model (canvas takes full screen) - Floating toolbar appears on selection or long-press - Bottom tool palette for drawing tools (thumb reachable) - Gesture-based manipulation (direct manipulation paradigm)

Mobile/Tablet (under 1024px): - Full-bleed canvas - Floating bottom tool palette (48px height) - Top-right: Undo/Redo + Menu - Selection shows floating inspector
Desktop (1024px and above): - Left sidebar: Layer panel (collapsible) - Right sidebar: Properties inspector (collapsible) - Bottom: Timeline/tool palette - Canvas fills remaining space

Touch Interactions: - Single tap: Select element - Double tap: Edit element text - Long press (500ms): Show context menu - Drag: Move selected or draw (depending on tool) - Two-finger pan: Pan canvas - Pinch: Zoom canvas - Two-finger rotate: Rotate selected element (when supported)
Mouse Interactions: - Click: Select - Right-click: Context menu - Drag: Move or draw - Scroll wheel: Zoom - Middle-click drag: Pan - Hover: Show resize handles on selected element
Keyboard Shortcuts: - Arrow keys: Nudge selected element (1px, Shift = 10px) - Delete/Backspace: Delete selected - Cmd/Ctrl+D: Duplicate - Cmd/Ctrl+Z: Undo - Space+Drag: Pan - V: Select tool - R: Rectangle tool - P: Pin tool

UI Library Decision: Custom + Radix UI primitives Reasoning: Canvas-based editors require complete custom rendering control. Radix UI for tool palette, menus, and dialogs (not canvas elements).
Components: - Canvas: Custom React component with Konva or custom canvas - ToolPalette: Radix UI Toolbar customized - ContextMenu: Radix UI ContextMenu - PropertiesPanel: Custom component - LayerPanel: Custom component with drag reordering - FloatingInspector: Custom animated component

Critical Issue: Pinch-to-zoom conflicts with browser zoom and two-finger swipe navigation
Resolution: - Canvas captures all touch events when in edit mode - Disable browser gestures when canvas is active - Provide explicit zoom UI controls as alternative - Clear visual feedback when gesture mode changes - Escape hatch: Button to exit edit mode and restore browser gestures

example.completion: Designed a touch-first canvas editor for floor plans with gesture support (pan, zoom, draw), keyboard shortcuts for power users, and explicit conflict resolution for gesture overlap. Used custom canvas rendering with Radix UI for tool chrome.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Canvas editors require explicit gesture mode management
Provide both gesture AND explicit UI controls for critical actions
Consider Apple Pencil + keyboard simultaneously on tablets
